coredata.dc:title: A visual method for determination of hepatitis C virus RNAs based on a 3D nanocomposite prepared from graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yifei
Li, Jingwen
Cao, Ya
Jiang, Ping
Tang, Yuguo
Chen, Zutao
Han, Kun
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive and specific assay of hepatitis C virus (HCV) RNA has a great clinical significance for the diagnosis of hepatitis C in window period. A colorimetric assay is described for determination of the RNA of hepatitis C virus. The method is based on the in situ synthesis of graphene quantum dot-silver nano-composites (GQD/Ag NCs), magnetic separation and catalyzed hairpin assembly reaction (CHA). The silver component of GQD/Ag NCs react with H2O2 generated by glucose oxidase, which are induced via CHA. Then the light-yellow solution turns into colorless transparent in the presence of the target RNAs. The method has a linear response in the 25–500 pM RNA concentration range, and the detection limit is 24.84 pM. The method is simple, stable and sensitive and might be extend to the assay of other RNA viruses. The retro-transcription free and visual distinguishable properties endow this strategy a promising application in the screening of HCV.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colorimetric assay
Graphene quantum dot-silver nano-composites
RNA Determination
Catalyzed hairpin assembly
RNA virus

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of reaction conditions for HCV RNA determination
Dynamics research of nucleic acid walker
Selectivity of the colorimetric sensing for HCV RNA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GQD/Ag NCs (A) UV–vis spectra and the photograph (Inset) of GQDs (a), GQD/Ag NCs (b) and GQD/Ag NCs + H2O2 (c). (B ∼ E) TEM of GQDs (B), GQD/Ag NCs (C) and GQD/Ag NCs after oxidization by H2O2 (D ∼ E).
UV–vis spectra and the photograph (Inset) of (a) H1+HCV RNA + GODx; (b) H1+HCV RNA + H2; (c) H1+H2+GODx; (d) H1+HCV RNA + H2+GODx.
UV–vis spectra of (a) MB-H1-HCV RNA (100%); (b) MB-H1 (50%), MB-H1-HCV RNA (50%).

(A) UV–vis spectra of GQDs/Ag NCs at different HCV RNA concentrations: 0, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500, 1000 and 2500 pM; (B) Plot fitting of absorbance at 425 nm. Plot of absorbance decrease versus HCV RNA concentration (25–500 pM).
Preparation of GQD/Ag NCs and the schema for HCV RNA ultrasensitive determination based on CHA and color change of GQD/Ag NCs.

head.ce:title: A visual method for determination of hepatitis C virus RNAs based on a 3D nanocomposite prepared from graphene quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Lab of Bio-Medical Diagnostics
Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive and specific assay of hepatitis C virus (HCV) RNA has a great clinical significance for the diagnosis of hepatitis C in window period. A colorimetric assay is described for determination of the RNA of hepatitis C virus. The method is based on the in situ synthesis of graphene quantum dot-silver nano-composites (GQD/Ag NCs), magnetic separation and catalyzed hairpin assembly reaction (CHA). The silver component of GQD/Ag NCs react with H2O2 generated by glucose oxidase, which are induced via CHA. Then the light-yellow solution turns into colorless transparent in the presence of the target RNAs. The method has a linear response in the 25–500 pM RNA concentration range, and the detection limit is 24.84 pM. The method is simple, stable and sensitive and might be extend to the assay of other RNA viruses. The retro-transcription free and visual distinguishable properties endow this strategy a promising application in the screening of HCV.

ce:section.ce:para: The 2019-nCoV are developed into the global pandemic and affecting people's social and economic activities seriously. One of the obstacles to control the situation is the limitation of techniques to promptly and sensitively identify the RNAs of viruses. Ultrasensitive and rapid determination of RNAs plays a critical role in the early diagnosis and screening of RNA virus induced infectious diseases. Hepatitis C virus (HCV) is a plus strand RNA virus, which belongs to family Flaviviridae. Infection of HCV may cause a series of liver diseases, such as liver fibrosis, cirrhosis even hepatocellular carcinoma [1–4]. HCV infection is one of the major causes of liver diseases in the world. According to statistics from WHO, 1.75 million people newly acquire hepatitis C virus infection worldwide in 2015 [5]. These people are at risk of a slow progression to severe liver disease and death, unless they receive timely testing and treatment. As a result, the sensitive diagnosis of HCV has attracted intense interests. Screening and diagnosis methods such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) can hardly detect HCV virus in the window phase of infection [6–11]. Clinically, serologic testing for antibodies and real time PCR are regarded as the gold standard for diagnosis of HCV infection, which had a delay due to the detection limit [12,13]. Therefore, a highly sensitive and specific method for the assay of HCV RNA has great clinical significance.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new type carbon nanomaterial with zero-dimensional graphene sheets, are smaller than 100 nm in transverse dimension and have unique sp2 and sp3 carbon structures [14]. GQDs have several particular physical and chemical properties, such as low cytotoxicity, fine solubility and convenient surface grafting and so on [15]. These properties endow GQDs various applications in bio-imaging [16,17], bio-sensing [18,19], electrochemical catalysis etc [20,21]. To date, GQDs have been employed as excellent support materials for silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) due to their stable chemical properties and large surface area [22–25]. Plenty of efforts have been contributed to the synthesis and application of the GQD/Ag NCs. Khaled et al. have reported a new synthesis method of polyethylene glycol GQD/Ag NCs, which showed unique antibacterial ability [26]. Akram and Mahmoud have revealed that doping GQD/Ag NCs on the electrode surface could greatly improve the electron transfer efficiency [27]. However, the application of the GQD/Ag NCs is still limited considering their harsh and time-consuming synthesis process. Chen et al. have re-ported a fast and facile approach to synthesize GQD/Ag complexes, which showed strong stability and perfectly solve problem of easy oxidation and agglomeration of silver nanoparticles [28]. The reported GQD/Ag NCs had excellent structure and morphology and better catalytic efficiency for the reduction of H2O2.

To meet the clinical requirements, various amplification technologies have been employed to improve the sensitivity, such as rolling circle amplification (RCA) [29,30], polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [31,32] and untemplated nucleic acid elongation [33,34]. However, these techniques are too sophisticated and time-consuming to be widely used in clinic applications. Catalyzed hairpin assembly has been proved to be effective and have a wonderful performance in signal amplification [35]. This protocol has been widely used in plenty of analytical techniques, such as fluorescence and electrochemistry. Colorimetric detection method is a conventional visualization technique without expensive and complicated instrument. Therefore, development of efficient, sensitive and convenient colorimetric detection has high research value. With the rapid development of DNA technology, a series of DNA nanomachines have been continuously developed. DNA walker can covert chemical energy into chemical energy, and move along a designed orbit. Fan et al. designed an exonuclease III stochastic DNA walker, and found that the high efficiency of exonuclease III towards spherical nucleic acid [36].

Herein, we report a 3D DNA nano-machine based on catalyzed assembly, magnetic separation and GQD/Ag NCs for sensitive determination of HCV RNA (Scheme 1 ). The presence of target, HCV RNA, will trigger the catalyzed hairpin assembly of hairpin H1 and H2 via hybridization reaction. H1/H2 complex cause the replacement of target HCV RNA, which is released and evokes the next circulation. After rounds of the target induced hybridization, streptavidin modified glucose oxidase (GODx) is introduce to the biotinylated H2 via biotin-streptavidin coupling. During the catalysis of glucose by the GODx, hydrogen peroxide is produced, which will then turn the light yellow GQD/Ag NCs solution into colorless transparent silver ions solution. This visual solution sensor shows promising performance in the determination of HCV RNAs.

Streptavidin-Glucose Oxidase (SA-GODx) was purchased from Fitzgerald Industries International (America). Graphene quantum dot (GQD), single layer graphene oxide (GO) and carboxylated magnetic beads were purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co. Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was purchased from Fitzgerald Industries (America). NH3•H2O, H2O2, AgNO3, 2-(N-Morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), NaCl, NaOH, HCl, Na2HPO4•12H2O, NaH2PO4•2H2O were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Two buffers were prepared in advance as follows: (a) Tris buffer: Tris 20 mM. NaCl 0.5 M, PH 8.0; (b) MES buffer: MES 25 mM, PH 6.0. All reagents are of analytical grade and were used without further purification. All solutions were prepared in Milli-Q water (>18.0 MΩ cm) from a Millipore system. DNA oligonucleotides were synthesized by Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and the sequences are listed as follows:
H1: 5′-NH2-TTTTTGTGGTCTGCGGAACCGGTGAGTACACAAGTGGTAG AGGATGTACTCACCGGTTCCG-3′
H2: 5′-Biotin-TGAGTACATCCTCTACCACTTGTGTACTCACCGGTGTGGTAGAGGA-3′
HCV RNA: 5′-UGUACUCACCGGUUCCGCAGACCAC-3′
HAV RNA: 5′-ACAAGGAGAACAGGGAACAUUCAGA-3′
C-RNA: 5′-UUGUACUACACAAAAGUACUG-3′

One milliliter (0.8 mg mL−1) of GQD was added with NH3•H2O to adjust PH 7.0. By dropping NH3•H2O into AgNO3 (50 mg mL−1), black precipitate was first produced and then disappeared to prepare fresh Toulon reagent. A volume of 10 μL of Toulon reagent was added to the prepared GO solution, shaken violently at room temperature for 30 min, and then, the mixed solution was heated at 100 °C for 60 min.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a U-3900H UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) with a 100 μL black quartz cell. Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) was performed with FEI TECNAI G2 F20 200 KV.

Carboxylated magnetic beads were added with the same volume of NH2-modified H1 DNA and incubated at room temperature for 0.5 h with gentle shaking. Subsequently, 20 μL of 10 mg mL−1 EDC solution was added and mixed evenly, shaking at room temperature for 2 h. Finally, coated magnetic beads were incubated with 0.5% (w/v) BSA solution at 37 °C for 0.5 h to block the unreactive carboxyl groups. The magnetic beads were washed and resuspended with MES buffer and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Hairpin H1 and hairpin H2 were designed according to the target HCV RNA sequence and CHA reaction principle. Before the start of CHA reaction, hairpin H1 and hairpin H2 were dissolved in Tris buffer, heated at 95 °C for 5 min, and slowly cooled to room temperature. Different concentrations of HCV RNA and 20 μL of H1-MB were reacted with 200 nM H2 at 37 °C for 90 min. After the CHA reaction was completed, the magnetic beads were separated and washed with MES buffer. Finally, adding 2 μL SA-GODx and incubating at 37 °C for 30 min, GODx was combined to the surface of magnetic beads by streptavidin-biotin and then separated and washed with MES buffer.

The procedures of colorimetric determination of HCV RNA were as follows: 80 μL solution contains 20 mM glucose, and 1 mg mL−1 as-prepared magnetic beads react at 37 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, 40 μL of GQD/Ag NCs was added and reacted at 37 °C for 30 min. The colorimetric results were analyzed by recording UV–vis spectra using a Hitachi U-3900H UV–vis spectrophotometer. The absorption spectrum of colorimetric determination ranges from 300 to 700 nm.

This HCV RNA assay protocol is based on the DNA walker amplification and GQD/Ag NCs (Scheme 1). Generally, it is divided into two parts: the signal part and the target induced amplification (TIA) part. For the signal part, a novel GQD/Ag NCs are proposed and synthesized, which have the absorption in certain wave length. The silver components react with H2O2 to form Ag+ and superoxide. Then, the superoxide subsequently reacts with GQD and quenched its fluorescence via an electron charging discharging mechanism [37]. For the signal amplification part, the H1 sequence functionalized magnetic beads react with the HCV RNA first and the hairpin structure of H1 is opened. The consequent binding of biotin-functionalized H2 with target induced opened H1 frees the HCV RNA, which will walk to the neighbor H1 and catalyze the next binding H2 with H1. After rounds of reactions, most of the H1 hybridize with H2 and the SA-GODx will bind to the H2 via biotin-streptavidin coupling. After washing and magnetic separation, the TIA part is ready. In the presence of glucose, the hydrogen peroxide, produced via GODx catalysis, will turn the GQD/Ag NCs into GQD and silver ions. The light yellow of the solution finally fade into colorless transparent.

In order to confirm the formation of the nano-composite, GQD/Ag NCs were characterized via UV–Vis spectrophotometer and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, GQD has no obvious absorption peak in the wavelength range of 300–700 nm (line a). After the in situ synthesis of Ag NCs on GQD surface, a new absorption peak at 425 nm appears (line b) due to the surface plasma excitation of the silver nano-particles. Compare with our previous research [38], the red shift may contribute to the existence of GQDs and the bigger size of the Ag NPs. The UV–Vis result indicates the formation of GQD/Ag NCs. We further studied the reaction of H2O2 and GQD/Ag NCs, and found that GQD/Ag NCs obviously faded in the presence of H2O2. As shown in line c, the absorption peak at 425 nm is significantly reduced, which provides great potential for quantitative determination.

Subsequently, The GQD and GQD/Ag NCs were characterized by TEM. The results are shown in Fig. 1(B ∼ E). The size of GQD ranges from 1.8 nm to 3 nm, with an average size of 2.13 nm (Fig. 1B). the size of GQD/Ag NCs ranges from 40 nm to 60 nm with an average size of 48.6 nm (Fig. 1C), which is larger than the previous report [39]. The possible reaction mechanism is that Ag(NH3)3+ adsorbed to GQD surface via electrostatic interaction. Attribute to the abundant carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups on the edge of GQD, GQDs promote the formation of Ag(NH3)2 on its surface [40], and Ag(NH3)2 maintains a temporary stable morphology in the presence of free NH3 molecule, then tends to form oligomers and silver nanoparticles colloids. In addition, the colloid of silver nanoparticles furtherly promotes the reduction of GQD and the in situ growth of silver nanoparticles on the surface of GQD. As shown in Fig. S1, FT-IR spectrum was used to confirm the reduction of Ag NCs. Obvious absorption bands at 3439, 1577 and 1048 cm−1 can be seen for GQDs, which are assigned to O–H, –NH2 and C–O stretching-vibration peaks, respectively. These peaks show that there are a large number of -OH, -NH2 and -COOH groups on the surface of GQDs. Compared GQDs/Ag NCs with the GQDs, the absorption band of O-H at 1400 cm−1, This result shows that Ag+ can be reduced by O-H groups on the surface of GQDs to form Ag NCs. Hydrogen peroxide was added to GQD/Ag NCs nano-composite solution. As shown in Fig. 1D, E, it could be found that a large number of nanoparticles about 2 nm are dispersed in the field of vision. We speculate that nano-composite in Fig. 1C were dissolved by hydrogen peroxide and returns to the form as GQDs. The coexistence of GQDs and larger nanoparticles (as shown in Fig. 1E) is also observed. The particle size is in the range of 8–12 nm, which is obviously much smaller than the nano-composite in Fig. 1C, which may contribute to the partially dissolution of the nano-composite by H2O2. All the TEM data demonstrate the feasibility of this nano-composite based signal system.

H2, HCV RNA and GODx are three key elements of this protocol. In order to better evaluate its feasibility, control experiments were designed. As shown in Fig. 2 (ãc), any one of the three elements is missing, there is hardly any change of the absorption peak of GQD/Ag NCs. Only when the three elements exist simultaneously, the reactions are able to take place in tandem as designed. Then 425 nm absorption peak of GQD/Ag NCs drops down obviously Fig. 2d. And the light-yellow solution turns into colorless transparent. Meanwhile, in the absence of H1, there is no change of the absorption peak of GQD/Ag NCs (Fig. S2), which confirms the immobilization of H1 on magnetic beads. Therefore, the protocol works well and is feasible to detect HCV RNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of reaction conditions for HCV RNA determination
ce:section.ce:para: In order to improve the performance of this protocol, the main factors that affecting the reaction are optimized, such as modification concentration of H1, amount of SA-GODx and the reaction time of CHA. As shown in Fig. S3A, with the increasing concentration of hairpin H1 modification, the absorption peak at 425 nm decreased significantly and reached the lowest point at 150 nM concentration of hairpin H1. The subsequent increase in hairpin H1 concentration will not have a significant impact on it. It indicates that the surface of the magnetic beads has been modified to saturation when the modification concentration is 150 nM, which is chosen in the subsequent experiments. Similarly, As can be seen from the absorption value varies little after 45 min reaction, indicating that the shortest time required for CHA reaction in this reaction system is 45 min (Fig. S3B). The minimum amount of SA-GODx 2 μL is required to reach the desired concentration (Fig. S3C). With the increase of glucose concentration, the absorbance at 425 nm decreased continuously. No obvious change occurred until the glucose concentration reached more than 20 mM (Fig. S3D). Hence, 20 mM of glucose was chosen as the optimal condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamics research of nucleic acid walker
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the high efficiency of CHA reaction on the surface of magnetic beads, HCV RNA on the surface of magnetic beads has the possibility of catalyzing H1 adjacent to the surface of other magnetic beads. In order to further prove that HCV RNA strand can react on the surface of adjacent magnetic bead systems, we designed two kinds of magnetic beads for determination: MB-H1-HCV RNA (50%) and MB-H1 (50%) [41]. If HCV RNA is able to walk between different magnetic beads as a catalytic chain adjacent to the magnetic beads, the peak decline of GQD/Ag NCs will be almost the same as the experimental results of MB-H1-HCV RNA (100%) system. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 3 . The overall reaction time is increased, and the peak reaches the lowest point at 425 nm at 75 min, and then no obvious decline occurs. Therefore, the data proves that HCV RNA can walk between different magnetic beads as a catalytic chain.

GODx oxidize glucose in the presence of molecular oxygen to produce D-gluconolactone while consuming oxygen to produce H2O2. Fig. 4 shows the determination results of HCV RNA at different concentrations in this protocol. Under the above optimized condition parameters, the absorption value at 425 nm decreases continuously, with the increasing HCV RNA concentration. This is consistent with the expected results. The data shows that the HCV RNA concentration had a fine linear relationship with the absorbance value in the range of 25–500 pM (R2 = 0.997), with detection limit (LOD) as low as 24.84 pM (Fig. 4). The fitting equation is y = 1.406-1.622x, where y represents the absorbance value at 425 nm and X means the concentration of HCV RNA. The sensitivity of the strategy meets clinical requirements.

A comparison with previous researches concerning the HCV diagnosis is also made (Table 1 ). Lin et al. developed a nanozyme-based bio-barcode amplified assay strategy [42]. Our group reported an HCV DNA sensing strategy based on DNA assisted magnetic reduced graphene oxide-copper nanocomposite. Both reports mainly target HCV DNA sequence, in which the retro-transcription is required. Also, Ghanbari's group developed an aptamer-sensor, taking HCV core protein as the biomarker, which has not been accepted in clinical guide [45]. The limit of detection is 3.3 pg mL-1, in nanomole level. The performance of this assay strategy is similar to Lin's (similar linear range and a bit higher LOD) and much better than the others. Compared to the reported method [12,13,42–45], our strategy has certain advantages: (a) obvious color differences for naked eye comparison; (b) target HCV RNA directly instead of DNA, (c) PCR free and reverse transcription free, (d) reaction taken in constant temperature (d) similar or better performance in LOD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the colorimetric sensing for HCV RNA
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of HCV RNA determination of this protocol is also evaluated. A Control-RNA (C-RNA), HAV RNA and their mixture were selected as comparison. Fig. S4 shows that the absorption value at 425 nm had no significant change when only HAV RNA (c) or C-RNA (d) is contained. Only when HCV RNA is present, either alone or with C-RNA and HAV RNA, the absorption value at 425 nm decreased significantly. These results indicate that C-RNA and HAV RNA have no significant impact on the determination of HCV RNA in this protocol. Therefore, this colorimetric sensing protocol based on CHA reaction and GQD/Ag NCs has good selectivity and stability.

In summary, we have developed and validated a sensitive colorimetric sensing protocol against HCV RNA was proposed and validated. This protocol based on the in-situ synthesis of GQD/Ag NCs, magnetic separation and CHA reaction. The existence of target RNA can be distinguished via the regression of light yellow of the solution. The method has a linear response in the 25–500 pM RNA concentration range, and the detection limit is 24.84 pM. The method directly targets the RNA sequence and no retro-transcription procession is in need. This protocol has a promising potential application in the screening of HCV. As the pretreatment of sample is still required, this is the main hindrance for the clinical application of this method. Also, this protocol might be extended to the assay of other RNA viruses which need to be further verified.

Yifei Li: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jingwen Li: Investigation, Validation. Ya Cao: Investigation, Methodology. Ping Jiang: Formal analysis, Investigation. Yuguo Tang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Supervision. Zutao Chen: Formal analysis, Resources. Kun Han: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by (a) the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No.: 31500805]; (b) Youth Innovation Promotion Association of the Chinese Academy of Sciences [2015261]; (c) “333 High-level Talents Training Project of Jiangsu Province”; (d) Science and Technology Program of Suzhou [SYG201508].
